title: Nonadiabatic Renormalization Group for Strongly Coupled Multiscale Quantum Systems
abstract: Complex quantum systems are often multiscale in nature with strong interactions between different scales. We present a novel idea: iteratively suppressing, rather than tracing out, the fast, high-energy degrees of freedom in strongly correlated quantum systems with multiple energy scales in a non-perturbative way, termed nonadiabatic renormalization group. This leads to a quantum geometric structure of a nested fiber bundle, in which each fiber of a layer is itself a fiber bundle of the next layer. The nonadiabatic renormalization group brings a new type of tensor network states that shares physical legs among ''sites'' and encodes quantum entanglement beyond conventional matrix product states. We demonstrate how to apply the nonadiabatic renormalization group to different types of problems, including an interacting boson model and ab initio quantum chemistry with interacting electrons.
summary: This paper proposes a new renormalization group method (NARG) for quantum systems with hierarchical energy scales. By iteratively suppressing high-energy degrees of freedom instead of tracing them out, it creates a new type of tensor network (LETTA) that captures entanglement through shared physical legs. The method proves highly efficient for both interacting boson models and electronic correlation in quantum chemistry.
main_problem: Efficiently modeling complex, multiscale quantum systems where multiple energy or time scales are strongly coupled, specifically overcoming the exponential scaling of computational resources and the failure of standard tracing-out or product-basis approaches.
main_result: The development of the Nonadiabatic Renormalization Group (NARG) and the Leg-Tied Tensor Ansatz (LETTA), which successfully capture significant electron correlation in hydrogen chains and achieve rapid convergence in interacting boson models.
method: An iterative approach called Nonadiabatic Renormalization Group (NARG) that suppresses rather than traces out high-energy degrees of freedom, resulting in a Leg-Tied Tensor Ansatz (LETTA) where physical legs are shared among sites to encode entanglement.
model_or_system: The method is applied to an interacting boson model with multiple modes and anharmonicity, as well as ab initio quantum chemistry models such as the hydrogen chain (H8) involving interacting electrons.
key_observables: Low-lying many-body eigenstates, correlation energy, and quantum geometric properties like Berry curvature and the quantum geometric tensor.
important_parameters: Energy/time scales (fast vs. slow), coupling regimes (strong vs. weak), and the number of retained adiabatic states (D).
main_assumptions: Assumes exponential convergence of truncated states for practical utility and requires managing expansion coefficients and global overlap matrices across different configurations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares MPS to the new LETTA architecture; Figure 2 illustrates NARG as a nested fiber bundle; Figure 3 shows eigenvalue convergence in a boson model; Figure 4 shows ground state energy capture in the H8 chain.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the failure of standard methods in strong coupling, proposes the NARG framework and the resulting LETTA tensor network, demonstrates the mathematical structure of nested fiber bundles, and validates the method using boson and quantum chemistry models.
why_interesting: It introduces a novel tensor network architecture (LETTA) specifically designed for multiscale entanglement, offering a potential alternative to MPS for systems where energy scales are strongly coupled, such as molecular dynamics or open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Schematic of NARG as a nested fiber bundle whereby the fiber of each layer is itself a fiber bundle.

FIG. 1. Schematic of the leg-tied tensor ansatz (LETTA) in comparison with the conventional matrix product states (MPS). Physical legs can be shared between (a) nearest neigh- bours and (b) next-nearest-neighbours. (d) A LETTA with peridic boundary condition.

FIG. 3. Low-lying eigenstates of an strongly interacting bo- son model with 20 modes. The lowerst 16 eigenvalues are shown, with a rapid convergence with respect to the number of retained adiabatic states D.

FIG. 4. NARG for ab initio quantum chemistry. Electronic correlation is gradually incorporated into the NARG by in- creasing the retained adiabatic states. More than 80% of the correlation energy in the hydrogen chain H8 is captured by D = 200.
